title: Consumer Protection Act 2019 — Key definitions and consumer rights
text: The Consumer Protection Act 2019 defines a consumer as any person who buys goods or hires services for a consideration, and includes any user of such goods or beneficiary of such services. A consumer does not include a person who obtains goods for resale or commercial purposes. The Act defines a complaint as any allegation in writing made by a complainant that a trader or service provider has adopted unfair trade practices, sold goods with defects, provided deficient services, charged prices in excess of the displayed price or agreed price, or offered goods or services which are hazardous to life and safety. Deficiency means any fault, imperfection, shortcoming or inadequacy in the quality, nature and manner of performance of a service. Defect means any fault, imperfection or shortcoming in the quality, quantity, potency, purity or standard of goods. Unfair trade practice means a trade practice which adopts unfair methods or deceptive practices including making false representations about the quality or standard of goods, falsely representing goods as new when they are old, making false claims regarding price reductions, and conducting fake prize schemes. Every consumer has the right to be protected against marketing of goods and services which are hazardous to life and property, the right to be informed about quality and price, the right to be assured access to goods at competitive prices, the right to seek redressal against unfair trade practices, and the right to consumer education.
